member.name: T:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.CommonDataPreprocessing.OversampledDataDetector
member.summary:
(Deprecated, to be removed, do not use.) This class deals with identifying when a dataset is oversampled. Oversampling happens when the data is stored with a given timbase_s, and then retreived at a higher frequency, in which case many systems will return data at the requested timebese_s even though the data is not stored with sufficient fidelity,
When plotted, oversampled datasets look like "step-ladders".

member.name: M:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.CommonDataPreprocessing.OversampledDataDetector.GetOversampledFactor(TimeSeriesAnalysis.TimeSeriesDataSet)
member.summary: Identify the oversampled factor of a TimeSeriesDataSet. The oversamples need to be evenly spread in the dataset. Subsets of oversamples in an otherwise non-oversampled dataset will not be taken into account here.
member.returns: the average distance between two unique values in the dataset arrays and a key index where the first new value occurs. If the dataset is not oversampled, the method returns (1,0)

member.param: value greater than 1 indicating that every Floor(n*factor) value of the orignal data will be transferred.

member.summary: Returns a copy of the dataset that is oversampled by the given factor.

member.returns: a downsampled copy, or null if operation failed. (Method will fail if no timestamps are given.)

member.name: M:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.ClosedLoopGainGlobalSearchResults.Add(TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.UnitParameters,TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.UnitDataSet,System.Double[],System.Double[],System.Int32)
member.summary: Add the result of one paramter study as part of the global search
member.param: paramters that have been studied

member.name: M:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.ClosedLoopGainGlobalSearchResults.GetBestModel
member.summary:
Determine the best model after finished the global search for the process gain of the pid-input, by evaluating all the saved KPIs in a structured way. This method includes some heuristics.
Remember that "excitation" in a closed-loop system can happen in noe of three ways: 1) a change in the disturbance signal 2) a change in the setpoint of the PID-controller or 3) a change in external input u 4) any combination of the above three.
It is assumed that the disturbance does not depend on the external u nor on the setpoint y, and this motivates searching for the minima in v2 and v3, respectively.

member.name: T:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.ClosedLoopUnitIdentifier
member.summary:
Identification that attempts to identify a unit model jointly with estimating the additive signal acting on the output(disturbance signal) yet is counter-acted by closed-loop (feedback)control, such as with PID-control.
The approach requires combining information in the measured output signal with the information in the manipulated variable(determined by active control) that is inputted into the process
In order to accomplish this estimation, the process model and the disturbance signal are estimated together.

member.name: M:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.ClosedLoopUnitIdentifier.Identify(TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.UnitDataSet,TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.PidParameters,System.Int32)
member.summary: Identify the unit model of a closed-loop system and the disturbance (additive output signal)
member.param: the unit data set, containing both the input to the unit and the output
if the setpoint of the control changes in the time-set, then the paramters of pid control need to be given.
the index of the PID-input to the unit model
member.returns: The unit model, with the name of the newly created disturbance added to the additiveInputSignals

member.summary: "look for the time-constant that gives the disturbance that changes the least"

member.summary: Try to model the effects of other non-pid inputs in a MISO-system, given an inital estimate of d_est and unitModel, when the gain of the pid-output is forced equat to pidProcGain
member.param: the estimated gain of the the pid-controlled input (which has input pidInputIdx)
the estimated disturbance
a prior estimate of the model for all inputs
what input in the unitDataSet is controlled by the pid-controller?
paramters of the pid-controller

member.name: M:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.ClosedLoopUnitIdentifier.Step1GlobalSearchLinearPidGain(TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.UnitDataSet,TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.PidParameters,System.Int32,TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.UnitModel,System.Double,System.Double,TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.FittingSpecs,System.Int32)
member.summary:
tries N=numberOfGlobalSearchIterations process gains of the unitModel in the range[minPidProcessGain,maxPidProcessGain], using a closed-loop simulation that includes the PID-model with paramters pidParams that acts on input pidInputIdx of the unit model.
The method also accepts an inital guess of the unit model, unitModel_run1
if Y_setpoint changes: looking to find the process gain that "decouples" d_est from Y_setpoint as much as possible. or antoher way to look at ti is that the output U with setpoint effects removed should be as decoupled form Y_setpoint.
member.param: The best previous estimate of the unitModel
member.returns: the best model and the step size used as a tuple

member.name: M:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.ClosedLoopUnitIdentifier.GuessSignOfProcessGain(TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.UnitDataSet,TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.PidParameters,System.Int32)
member.summary:
Guess the sign of the pid-input
Look at the correlation between u and y. assuming that the sign of the Kp in PID controller is set correctly so that the process is not unstable: If an increase in _y(by means of a disturbance)_ causes PID-controller to _increase_ u then the processGainSign is negative If an increase in y causes PID to _decrease_ u, then processGainSign is positive!
member.returns: returns 1 if gain is positive or -1 if gain is negative.

member.name: T:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.FittingInfo
member.summary:
FittingInfo
Be careful as the objective function is different for the static estimation that considers the absolute values, while dynamic estimation considers "diffs"- for this reason it is best to use RsqDiff and RsqAbs when comparing different model runs which can be a combination fo static and dynamic

member.name: P:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.FittingInfo.WasAbleToIdentify
member.summary: True if identification was able to identify, otherwise false. Note that this flag is not an indication that the model is good, i.e. that the data had sufficient information to determine unique paramters that describe the dataset well. This flag only indicates that regression did not crash during identification.

member.name: P:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.FittingInfo.SolverID
member.summary: A string that identifies the solver that was used to find the model

member.name: P:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.FittingInfo.FitScorePrc
member.summary: A score that is 100% if model describes all variations and 0% if model is no better at describing variation than the flat average line. Negative if the model is worse than a flat average line.

member.name: P:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.FittingInfo.NFittingBadDataPoints
member.summary: Number of bad data points ignored during fitting

member.name: P:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.FittingInfo.NFittingTotalDataPoints
member.summary: Number of total data points (good and bad) available for fitting

member.name: P:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.FittingInfo.StartTime
member.summary: Start time of fitting data set

member.name: P:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.FittingInfo.EndTime
member.summary: End time of fitting data set

member.name: P:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.FittingInfo.RsqDiff
member.summary:
The value of the R2 or root mean square of fitting,higher is better (used to choose among models)
This is the R-squared of the "differences" sum(ymeas[k]-ymeas[k-1] -(ymod[k]-ymod[k-1]) ) >

member.name: P:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.FittingInfo.RsqAbs
member.summary:
The value of the R2 or root mean square
This is the R-squared of the "absolute" sum(ymeas[k] - ymod[k] ) >

member.name: P:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.FittingInfo.ObjFunValDiff
member.summary:
The value of the objective function during fitting, lower is better(used to choose among models)
This is the R-squared of the "differences" sum(ymeas[k]-ymeas[k-1] -(ymod[k]-ymod[k-1]) ) >

member.name: P:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.FittingInfo.ObjFunValAbs
member.summary:
The value of the objective function during fitting, lower is better(used to choose among models)
This is the R-squared of the "absolute" sum(ymeas[k]-ymod[k-1]) >

Takes as an input a model that already has a threshold for the linear gains that has been determined by other means, and then evalutes if model match improves if using two -2- different time-constants.

member.name: M:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.GainSchedIdentifier.IdentifyGainScheduledGainsAndSingleThresholdV2(TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.UnitDataSet,System.Int32,System.Boolean,System.Int32,System.Nullable{System.Double},System.Nullable{System.Double})
member.summary:
Perform a global search ("try-and-evaluate") for the threshold or thresholds that result in the gain-scheduling model with the best fit
Unlike V1, this method calls the IdentifyForGivenThresholds(), based on the observation that this method has quite good performance.
Note also that the operating point or bias is not set in the returned list of parameters. and that time-delays are not considered yet (this is done as a final step.)
member.param: determine the operating point/bias (true by default)
number of global search iterations
minimum of the global search(used for pass 2+)
maximum of the global search(used for pass 2+)
member.returns: a tuple of: a) A list of all the candiate gain-scheduling parameters that have been found by global search, but note that these models do not include operating point, and b) the simulated output of each of the gain-scheduled paramters in the first list.

member.name: M:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.GainSchedIdentifier.IdentifySingleLinearModelForGivenThresholds(TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.UnitDataSet@,System.Double[],System.Double[],System.Int32,System.Nullable{System.Double},System.Boolean)
member.summary:
When a upper/lower threshold is given, estimate local linear model for a dataset.
Note that if there is no variation in dataSet between uMinFit and uMaxFit, then this method may actually expand these variables out internally.
member.param: the lower threshold of the gain-scheduled input
the upper threshold of the gain-scheduled input
index of the variable in the model that is gain-scheduled. by the fault 0
if set to null, the u between uLowerThreshold and uHigherThreshold is used
if set to true, identification also considers time delay (increases computational load, use sparingly)
member.returns: return a tuple, the first linear gains is null if unable to identify

member.name: T:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.GainSchedTimeDelayIdentifier
member.summary:
Brute-force(trial and error) estimation of time delay
This method is intended to be generic, so that it can be applied on different kinds of process models, and thus it uses interfaces and dependency injection.
The idea of the method is to re-identify the model first with no time-delay, and then increasing the time-delay step-by-step. This process should continue so long as the model improves (as measured by uncertainty mangitude, objective function value or Rsquared).
Thus, this method is a component in reducing the problem of determining continous paramters along with the integer time delay (a mixed-integer problem) to a sequential optimization approach where the integer and continous parts of the problem are solved sequentially.

member.name: M:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.GainSchedTimeDelayIdentifier.GetR2DiffList
member.summary: Get R2 of all runs so far in an array,
member.returns: array or R-squared values, with Nan if any runs have failed

member.name: T:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.UnitIdentifier
member.summary:
Identifier of the "Default" process model - a dynamic process model with time-constant, time-delay, linear process gain and optional (nonlinear) curvature process gains.
This model class is sufficent for real-world linear or weakly nonlinear dynamic systems, yet also introduces the fewest possible parameters to describe the system in an attempt to avoid over-fitting/over-parameterization.
The "default" process model is identified using a linear-in-parameters parameterization (parameters a,b,c), so that it can be solved by linear regression and identification should thus be both fast and stable. The issue with the parameterization (a,b,c) is that the meaning of each parameter is less intuitive, for instance the time constant depends on a, but linear gain depends on both a and b, while curvature depends on a and c. Looking at the unceratinty of each parameter to determine if the model should be dynamic or static or what the uncertainty of the time constant is, is very hard, and this observation motivates re-parameterizing the model after identification.
When assessing and simulating the model, parameters are converted into the more intuitive parameters "time constant", "linear gains" and "curvature gain" which are a different parameterization. The UnitIdentifier, UnitModel and UnitParameters classes handle this transition seamlessly to the user. Uncertainty is expressed in terms of this more intuitive parameterization, to allow for a more intuitive assessment of the parameters.
Another advantage of the parameterization is that the model internally separates between steady-state and transient state. You can at any instance "turn off" dynamics and request the steady-state model output for the current input. This is useful if you have transient data that you want to analyze in the steady-state, as you can then fit the model to all available data-points without having to select what data points you believe are at steady state, then you can disable dynamic terms to do a static analysis of the dynamic model.
Time-delay is an integer parameter, and finding the time-delay alongside continuous parameters turns the identification problem into a linear mixed-integer problem. The time delay identification is done by splitting the time-delay estimation from continuous parameter identification, turning the solver into a sequential optimization solver. This logic to re-run estimation for multiple time-delays and selecting the best estimate of time delay is deferred to .
Since the aim is to identify transients/dynamics, the regression is done on model differences rather than absolute values.

member.name: T:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.UnitTimeDelayIdentifier
member.summary:
Brute-force(trial and error) estimation of time delay
This method is intended to be generic, so that it can be applied on different kinds of process models, and thus it uses interfaces and dependency injection.
The idea of the method is to re-identify the model first with no time-delay, and then increasing the time-delay step-by-step. This process should continue so long as the model improves (as measured by uncertainty mangitude, objective function value or Rsquared).
Thus, this method is a component in reducing the problem of determining continous paramters along with the integer time delay (a mixed-integer problem) to a sequential optimization approach where the integer and continous parts of the problem are solved sequentially.

member.name: T:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.PidController
member.summary:
Proporitional-Integral-Derivative(PID) controller
that supports
first and second order low pass filtering of process variable
anti-windup
bumpless transfer between auto and manual mode
"warmstarting" -bumpless startup
feedforward
scaling of input and output values
gain scheduling of Kp
gain scheduling of Ti
"kicking" as is usually applied to compressor recycling controllers/anti-surge
min select/max select (also referred to as high select or low select: (multiple pid-controllers controlling the same output switch between auto and tracking mode)
By design decision, this class should be kept relativly simple in terms of coding patterns, so that it is possible to hand-port this class to other languages (c++/c/Structured Text/Labview/Matlab etc). To simulate PID-control, use the wrapper class PIDModel, as it wraps this class and impelments the ISimulatableModel interface needed to simulate with ProcessSimulator

member.summary: Set the control to autoamtic mode (i.e. u varies based on inputs and settings) (use SetManualMode to switch back)

member.name: M:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.PidController.SetTrackingOffset(System.Double,System.Double)
member.summary: Set the offset that is to be added or subtracte to a split range controller that is inactive or tracking. If this value is above zero, then the controller is MIN SELECT, if this value is negative then the controller is assumed MAX SELECT if this vlaue is zero, then trakcking will not work properly as controller will be unable to determine if its output was selected by looking at the tracking signal.

member.name: M:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.PidController.Iterate(System.Double[],System.Double[],System.Double[])
member.summary: Calculates an entire output vector u given vector of processes and setpoints (and optionally a tracking signal for split range) This method is useful for back-testing against historic data.

member.name: M:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.PidController.Iterate(System.Double,System.Double,System.Nullable{System.Double},System.Nullable{System.Double},System.Nullable{System.Double})
member.summary: Calculates the next u[k] output of the controller given the most recent process value and setpoint value, and optionally also including the tracking signal (only applicable if this is a split range controller) and optionally the gainScehduling variable if controller is to gain-schedule

member.name: M:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.PidController.GetUWithoutTracking
member.summary: Split range controllers add an offset to the output u for inactive or "tracking" controllers. This function returns the "raw" u without tracking, which can be useful for initalizing simulations with split range control.

member.name: M:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.PidController.GetUIfInAuto
member.summary: Returns the value of the output u that the controller would give if it was in auto. This is useful when considering turning on a controller that is in manual to see that the controller gives a sensible output. Call "iterate" first to update the internals of the controller.

member.summary: Initalizes the controller internal state(integral term) to be steady at the given process value and output value, useful to avoid bumps when staring controller

member.name: T:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.PlantSimulator
member.summary:
Simulates larger "plant-models" that is built up of connected sub-models that each implement ISimulatableModel.
To set up a simulation, first connect models, and then add external input signals. This class handles information about which model is connected to which, and handles calling sub-models in the correct order with the correct input signals.
By default, the model attempts to start in steady-state, intalization handled by ProcessSimulatorInitializer (this requires no user interaction).
The building blocks of plant models are UnitModel, PidModel and Select

member.name: F:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.PlantSimulator.restartModelAfterXConsecutiveBadIndices
member.summary: How many consequtive bad indices are required before the simulator does a new "warm-up" on the first following good indice. should be larger than two, because a single bad point can can require skipping two indices in a recursive model, and thus can happen often.

member.name: M:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.PlantSimulator.Simulate(TimeSeriesAnalysis.TimeSeriesDataSet,TimeSeriesAnalysis.TimeSeriesDataSet@)

member.returns: true if able to simulate, otherwise false

member.name: M:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.PlantSimulator.Simulate(TimeSeriesAnalysis.TimeSeriesDataSet,System.Boolean,TimeSeriesAnalysis.TimeSeriesDataSet@)
member.summary:
Perform a "plant-wide" full dynamic simulation of the entire plant,i.e. all models in the plant, given the specified connections and external signals.
The dynamic simulation will also return estimated disturbances in simData, if the plant contains feedback loops where there is an additive disturbance with a signal named according to SignalNamer.EstDisturbance() convention
The simulation will also set the PlantFitScore which can be used to evalute the fit of the simulation to the plant data. For this score to be calculated, the measured time-series corresponding to simData need to be provided in inputData
If doDetermineIndicesToIgnore is set to false, then the the simulation will consider the .IndicesToIgnore member of the inputData, and ignore these data indices, re-starting dynamic models once periods of bad data pass.(this indcies will be padded internally do not need to be padded when supplied) If instead set to true, the simulator will parse the input data to determine bad indices, but it will not be able to determine periods of "frozen" datasets (as outputs y are generally not required to be given in the input data.)
member.param: the external signals for the simulation(also, determines the simulation time span and timebase)
is set to true, the simulator tries to determine indices to ignore internally
the simulated data set to be outputted(excluding the external signals)

member.name: M:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.PlantSimulator.SelectMinimalInputData(TimeSeriesAnalysis.TimeSeriesDataSet)
member.summary: Choose only the input time series that are actually used by a given plant

member.summary:
Simulate single model based on a unit data set
This is a convenience function that creates a TimeSeriesDataSet, sets default names in the model and dataset that match based on unitDataset The output is returned directly.
Optionally, the result can be written to y_meas or y_sim in unitdata.

model to simulate

member.name: T:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.PlantSimulatorInitalizer
member.summary:
Intializes a plant simulator in the first data point
Currently, only initalizing to steady-state is supported.
By design choice, this class traverses the models by logic to initialize the plant model rather than using mathematical programming/matrix solvers.

member.name: M:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.PlantSimulatorInitalizer.#ctor(TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.PlantSimulator)
member.summary: Constructor
member.param: simulator object that already includes connections,models and signals to be simulated

member.name: M:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.PlantSimulatorInitalizer.ToSteadyStateAndEstimateDisturbances(TimeSeriesAnalysis.TimeSeriesDataSet@,TimeSeriesAnalysis.TimeSeriesDataSet@,System.Collections.Generic.Dictionary{System.String,System.Collections.Generic.List{System.String}})
member.summary:
Initalize the empty datasets to their steady-state values
Forward-calculate all processes in series (not in any feedback-loops), where inputs to leftmost process is given
All PID-loops must have a setpoint value set and all Y_meas are initalized to setpoint
Then all subprocesses inputs inside feedback-loops U are back-calculated(right-to-left) to give steady-state Y_meas equal to Y_set
Finally determine if there are any serial processes downstream of any loops that can be calucated left-to-right
member.param: non-simulated time-series external inputs
simulation dataset containing only the external signals. The new simulated variables are added to this variable with initial values.
dictionary of all computational loops

member.name: M:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.PlantSimulatorInitalizer.InitComputationalLoops(System.Collections.Generic.Dictionary{System.String,System.Collections.Generic.List{System.String}},System.Collections.Generic.Dictionary{System.String,System.Double}@,System.Collections.Generic.List{System.String}@)
member.summary: Try to initalize compulatational loops
member.param: dictionary of computataio

member.name: M:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.PlantSimulatorInitalizer.EstimateDisturbances(TimeSeriesAnalysis.TimeSeriesDataSet@,TimeSeriesAnalysis.TimeSeriesDataSet@,System.Collections.Generic.Dictionary{System.String,System.Double}@)
member.summary: For a plant, go through and find each plant/pid-controller and attempt to estimate the disturbance. For the disturbance to be estimateable,the inputs "u_meas" and the outputs "y_meas" for each "process" in each pid-process loop needs to be given in inputData. The estimated disturbance signal is added to simData
member.param: note that for closed loop systems u and y signals are removed(these are used to estimate disturbance, removing them triggers code in PlantSimulator to re-estimate them)
member.returns: true if everything went ok, otherwise false

member.name: M:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.PlantSimulatorInitalizer.BackwardsCalcFeedbackLoops(System.Collections.Generic.Dictionary{System.String,System.Double}@,System.Collections.Generic.List{System.String}@)
member.summary: Initalizes sub-processes inside PID-feedback loops "from right-to-left"(backwards,finds for a given y what is u) This method only supports a single PID-input per model.

member.name: M:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.PlantSimulatorInitalizer.ForwardCalcNonPID(System.Collections.Generic.Dictionary{System.String,System.Double}@)
member.summary:
Calculates "from left-to-right"(forward, so from input to output) interconnected models where these are connected in series, where the first model in the chain only has external inputs.
This method does not initalize PID-controllers or inside control loops.

member.name: M:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.PlantSimulatorInitalizer.SetPidControlledVariablesToSetpoints(System.Collections.Generic.Dictionary{System.String,System.Double}@)
member.summary:
Method that initalizes PID-controllers
It does not atttempt to initalize min-select controllers or cascade controllers, this is deferred to other methods
member.returns: the IDs of the PID-controllers the method did not initalize

member.name: M:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.PlantSimulatorInitalizer.SelectLoopsCalc(System.Double,System.Collections.Generic.Dictionary{System.String,System.Double}@,System.Collections.Generic.List{System.String}@)
member.summary: Initalizes PID controllers inside min/max select loops
member.para: Which controller is active inside select loops depends on the value of the disturbance on the output of the modelled process

member.name: T:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.GainSchedModel
member.summary:
Simulatable gain-scheduled model.
A model for systems that cannot be adequately modelled by UnitModel,because they either have time constants or gains or both that vary signficantly depending on the value of one of the inputs.
One input is selected as the "scheduling varible" and one ore more thresholds are given for this scheduling variable. The thresholds can be set indepently for time-contant and linear gain.
Remember that with more thresholds defined, the higher the requirement for information content in data will be if the model is to be identified from it.
This should not be confused with "gain-scheduled" PID-control, which is a similar concept but applied to PID-control parameters.
See also:

member.name: T:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.PidModel
member.summary:
Simulatable industrial PID-controller
This class is as a wrapper for class, that implements .
To simulate minimum or maximum select controllers, combine this class with blocks.
The controller paramters belong to different aspects of the controller like, tuning, scaling, gain-scheduling, feedforward and anti-surge are adjusted have been collected into a number of data-classes, linked below:

member.name: T:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.UnitModel
member.summary:
Simulatable "default" process model.
This is a model that can be either dynamic or static, have one or multiple inputs and can be either linear in inputs or have inputs nonlinearity described by a second-order polynominal. Dynamics can be either 1. order time-constant, time-delay or both. The model also supports "additive" signals added to its output (intended for modeling disturbances).
The model is designed to lend itself well to identification from industrial time-series datasets, and is supported by the accompanying identification method .
This model is also intended to be co-simulated with by to study process control feedback loops.
It is assumed that most unit processes in industrial process control systems can be described sufficiently by this model, and thus that larger plants can be modeled by connecting unit models based on this model structure.
It would be possible to extend this model to also describe second-order dynamics along the same principles by the introduction of one additional parameter in future work.
See also:

the value of the input

member.name: M:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.UnitModel.Iterate(System.Double[],System.Double,System.Double)
member.summary: Iterates the process model state one time step, based on the inputs given.
member.param: vector of inputs U. Optionally the output disturbance D can be added as the last value.
the time in seconds between samples
value in U that is to be treated as NaN
member.returns: the updated process model state (x) - the output without any output noise or disturbance. NaN is returned if model was not able to be identfied, or if no good U values have been given yet. If some data points in U inputs are NaN or equal to badValueIndicator, the last good value is returned

member.summary: Solve quadratic equation "a x^2 + b*x +c =0" (second order polynomial equation in a single variable x) x = [ -b +/- sqrt(b^2 - 4ac) ] / 2a

member.name: T:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.UnitParameters
member.summary: Parameters data class of the .

member.name: P:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.UnitParameters.LinearGains
member.summary: An array of gains that determine how much in the steady state each input change affects the output(multiplied with (u-u0))

member.name: P:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.UnitParameters.LinearGainUnc
member.summary: An array of 95% uncertatinty in the linear gains (u-u0))

member.name: P:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.UnitParameters.Curvatures
member.summary: The nonlinear curvature of the process gain, this paramter is multiplied + Curvatures*((u-u0)/Unorm)^2. If value is nullc> then no curvatures are added to the model

member.name: P:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.UnitParameters.CurvatureUnc
member.summary: The unceratainties of the curvature term of the process gains

member.name: P:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.UnitParameters.TimeConstant_s
member.summary: A time constant in seconds, the time a 1. order linear system requires to do 63% of a step response. Set to zero to turn off time constant in model.

member.name: P:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.UnitParameters.TimeConstantUnc_s
member.summary: The uncertinty of the time constant estimate

member.name: P:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.UnitParameters.TimeDelay_s
member.summary: The time delay in seconds.This number needs to be a multiple of the sampling rate. Set to zero to turn off time delay in model.

member.name: P:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.UnitParameters.DampingRatio
member.summary: Damping (second-order) values between ~0.3-0.99 will cause step response with a single visibl overshoot. ) Set to zero to disable damping. As values less than 0.3 approach zero, the step response will become more and more oscillatory.

member.name: P:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.UnitParameters.Bias
member.summary: The constant bias that is added so that models and dataset match on average, this value will depend on U0 and other parameters.

member.name: P:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.UnitParameters.BiasUnc
member.summary: The 95% uncertainty of the bias

member.name: P:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.UnitParameters.U0
member.summary: The working point of the model, the value of each U around which the model is localized. If value is nullc> then no U0 is used in the model.

member.name: P:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.UnitParameters.UNorm
member.summary: A "normal range" of U that is used in the nonlinear curvature term ((u-u0)/Unorm)^2. If value is nullc> then no UNorm is used in the model.

member.name: F:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.UnitParameters.Y_min
member.summary: The minimum allowed output value(if set to NaN, no minimum is applied)

member.name: F:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.UnitParameters.Y_max
member.summary: the maximum allowed output value(if set to NaN, no maximum is applied)

member.name: F:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.UnitParameters.FittingSpecs
member.summary: User-specified inputs to model fitting process such as minima,maxima and working point

member.summary: There are several eqvivalently good time delay estiamtes when judged by Rsquared.

member.summary: There are other good time delay estimates based on Rsquared that are not near the best value This is an indication that something is wrong.

member.summary: There are several eqvivalently good time delay estiamtes when judged by objective function value.

member.summary: There are other good time delay estimates based on objective function value that are not near the best value This is an indication that something is wrong.

member.name: F:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.ProcessTimeDelayIdentWarnings.SomeModelRunsFailedToFindSolution
member.summary: Warning that some of the model runs (i.e. for certain time delays) failed to yield a solution. This could be because there is very little information in the dataset, and if most of the information is at the very start this warning can arise as to test longer and longer time delays, a shorter and shorter portion of the dataset is used(some data at the very start needs to be "cut off")

member.name: F:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Dynamic.ProcessTimeDelayIdentWarnings.FallbackToLinearStaticModel
member.summary: Warning that estimation had to fall back on the static/linear model because other model runs failed.

member.returns: a

member.name: T:TimeSeriesAnalysis.BandPass
member.summary:
Numerical band-pass filter based on
This filter is in a recursive(feedback) IIR form that is simple to implement, has few coefficients, requires litte memory and computation. This filter is causal, meaning that for calculating the filtered value at time k it does not use future values such as k+1, but this is at the expense of introducing a time-shift/phase-shift.

member.param: data point
low-passfilter time constant in seconds
high-passfilter time constant in seconds
filter order, either 1 or 2 is supported
usually false, setting to true causes filter to reset to the value of signal

member.summary: Filter an entire time-series in one command
member.param: the vector of the entire time-series to be filtered
filter time constant
filter time constant
filter order, either 1 or 2
member.returns: a vector of the filtered time-series

member.name: T:TimeSeriesAnalysis.MovingAvg
member.summary:
Moving-average low-pass filter
This filter is causal, meaning that for calculating the filtered value at time k it does not use future values such as k+1, this is at the expense of introducing a time-shift/phase-shift.
This is a finite-impulse-response type filter.
An alterntive to this filter is , which is infinite-impulse-repsonse, and also requires less working memory(this filter needs to hold a buffer equal to bufferSize, but only needs to keep its last value in memory). will have less phase-shift/time-shift, because it places most weight on the last datapoint, whereas this filter will weight all data points in its buffer equally and thus responds sluggish.
The advantage of this filter is that it allows you to control precisely how many past values are weighted.

member.name: M:TimeSeriesAnalysis.MovingAvg.#ctor(System.Int32)
member.summary: Constructor
member.param: the number of samples to average, which determines the size of the buffer to create

member.param: the scalar value to be added
member.returns: the output of the filter, given the new value

member.summary: Run filter over a vector of values
member.param: vector of values to be filtered
member.returns: the moving-averge filtered version of values

member.name: M:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Vec.Regress(System.Double[],System.Double[0:,0:],System.Int32[])
member.summary: Robust linear regression.
member.param: vector of response variable values (to be modelled)
2D matrix of manipulated values/independent values/regressors used to explain Y
(optional) a list of the indices of values in Y to ignore in regression. By default it is null
member.returns: an object of the RegressionResult class with the parameters, as well as some statistics on the fit and uncertainty thereof.

member.name: M:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Vec.RegressUnRegularized(System.Double[],System.Double[][],System.Int32[])
member.summary: Regression that does not attempt to regualarize inputs toward zero.

member.name: M:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Vec.RegressRegularized(System.Double[],System.Double[][],System.Int32[],System.Collections.Generic.List{System.Int32})
member.summary: Robust linear regression, regularized. To avoid parameters taking on extremely high values in the case of a little excitation in the inputs, two mitigating actions are implemented by the solver to be "robust": - a "robust" Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) -based solver is used. - a regularization term is added to the objective function that will bring parameters to zero if (Y,X) does not contain any information to force the parameter away from zero.
member.param: vector of output variable values to be modelled
jagged 2D matrix of manipulated values/independent values/regressors used to explain Y
(optional) a list of the indices of values in Y to ignore in regression. By default it is null
(optional) only the indices in this list are to be regularized to zero
member.returns: an object of the RegressionResult class with the parameters, as well as some statistics on the fit and uncertainty thereof.

member.name: M:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Vec.Regress(System.Double[],System.Double[][],System.Int32[],System.Collections.Generic.List{System.Int32},System.Boolean)
member.summary: Robust linear regression. To avoid parameters taking on exteremly high values in the case of a little excitation in the inputs, two mitigating actions are implemented by the solver to be "robust": - a "robust" Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) -based solver is used. - a regularization term is added to the objective function that will bring parameters to zero if (Y,X) does not contain any information to force the parameter away from zero.
member.param: vector of output variable values to be modelled
jagged 2D matrix of manipulated values/independent values/regressors used to explain Y
(optional) a list of the indices of values in Y to ignore in regression. By default it is null
(optional) only the indices in this list are to be regularized to zero
(optional) adds a minor "regularization" term to objective that will pull values toward zero if corresponding input is not excited
member.returns: an object of the RegressionResult class with the parameters, as well as some statistics on the fit and uncertainty thereof.

member.name: M:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Vec.RSquared(System.Double[],System.Double[],System.Collections.Generic.List{System.Int32},System.Int32)
member.summary: R-squared. R-squared (R2) is a statistical measure that represents the proportion of the variance for a dependent variable that's explained by an independent variable or variables in a regression model. Whereas correlation explains the strength of the relationship between an independent and dependent variable, R-squared explains to what extent the variance of one variable explains the variance of the second variable. So, if the R2 of a model is 0.50, then approximately half of the observed variation can be explained by the model's inputs.
member.param: first vector
second vector
optionally: indices to be ignored (for instance bad values)
set the offset beteen vector1 and vector2 (for difference equations, ymod is offset by -1 from ymeas). -1 is default.
member.returns: R2 squared, a value between -1 and 1. If an error occured, Double.PositiveInfinity is returned.

member.summary: Smooths the array without phase-shifting by using both past and future values (i.e. so called non-causal smoothing).

member.name: T:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Utility.ParserFeedback
member.summary:
Utility class is responsible for collecting feedback lines, such as warnings,error or info text to either the console window, visual-studio output/debug window, to a file structure or all.
The class makes it easy to switch between displaying output to a console while debugging while to outputting to file when code moves to a server. Suitable for collecting debugging info from services that run many cases repeatedly.
log levels: INFO,WARN,ERROR,FATAL (no debug messages here)

member.name: T:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Utility.Plot
member.summary:
Static methods for plotting one or more time-series across one or more y-axes and one or more subplots by opening up Chrome calling a set of JavaScript plotting methods based on plotly.js
Time-series are written as CSV-files to a specific folder on disk plotDataPath, before starting a chrome-window that loads a specific "localhost" plotlyURL that reads said CSV-files and initates an in-browser plot using plotly.js.
For this method to work you need to be running a web server such as IIS that serves up a "localhost" plotlyURL as described in the documentation.
If you sometimes need to disable plots (for instance if plotting code is included in unit tests) see Plot4Test

member.name: M:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Utility.Plot.FromList(System.Collections.Generic.List{System.ValueTuple{System.Double[],System.DateTime[]}},System.Collections.Generic.List{System.String},System.String,System.String,System.Boolean)
member.summary: Plot data from a list of value-date tuples (each time-series can have unique time-vector with unique sampling)

member.name: M:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Utility.Plot.FromList(System.Collections.Generic.List{System.Double[]},System.Collections.Generic.List{System.String},System.DateTime[],System.String,System.String,System.Boolean)
member.summary: Plot all variables in a list of doubles, that all have the same timestamps given by dataTimes
member.param: common timestamps

member.name: M:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Utility.Plot.FromList(System.Collections.Generic.List{System.Double[]},System.Collections.Generic.List{System.String},System.Double,System.String,System.DateTime,System.String,System.Boolean)
member.summary: Plot values in a list of vectors dataList, when all vectors start at t0 and have a stedy sampling rate
member.param: List of doubles, one entry for each time-series to be plotted
List of string of unique names to describe each plot, prefixed by either "y1="(top left),"y2="(top right),"y3="(bottom left) or "y4="(bottom right) to denote what y-axis to plot the variable on
the time between data samples in seconds
a comment that is shown in the plot
the DateTime of the first data point
give each plot a casename if creating multiple plots with the re-occurring variable names
By setting doStartChrome to false, you can skip opening up chrome, the link to figure will instead be returned
member.returns: The url of the resulting plot is returned

member.name: T:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Utility.Plot4Test
member.summary: Version of Plot class where plots code can be Enabled()/Disabled() programatically. This allows you to keep all your "Plot" calls in your unit tests and turn them on as needed to debug a single test, while avoid being overwhelmed with plots if for instance re-running all unit tests.

member.name: M:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Utility.Plot4Test.#ctor(System.Boolean,System.Int32)
member.summary: Determine wheter plots are to be enabled or disabled on. To further prevent thousands of plots from accidentally being created by this object a maximum number of plots is set, override as needed.

member.name: M:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Utility.Plot4Test.Disable

member.name: M:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Utility.Plot4Test.Enable
member.summary: Enable all subsequent calls to plot using the same instance of this class.

member.name: T:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Utility.SigmaXml
member.summary: Class that concerns reading the "cache.js" file that Sigma generates, that contains time-series data.

member.name: M:TimeSeriesAnalysis.Utility.SigmaXml.LoadFromFile(System.String)
member.summary: Load the sigma "cache" file format, often named "cache.js". This file is json file, but is converted to a xml-file by replacing the header, and is read from javascript in json format and is also read from c# in the xml-converted format. The data has both a "cache" and an "eventdata" portion, but this driver reads only the cache format where the time-series data is usually stored.
